matchedGlosses: a primordial orisha primarily worshipped as an ancestral spirit by the Ìjẹ̀bú people. They are believed to be an orisha of fertility, childbirth, and protector of children. It is closely associated with Egúngún, Gẹ̀lẹ̀dẹ́, and Èlùkú.

previewGlosses: Oko (orisha), the orisha of farming and agriculture, in his male form, he is regarded as a sorcerer. The root, oṣó (“sorcerer”) in many names among the Ẹ̀gbá and Ìjẹ̀bú refers to Oko.

matchedGlosses: a type of long-necked bird with a crest; black crowned crane
giraffe (traditionally, giraffes did not live in the range of Yorubaland)
entries.note: Not a semantic mismatch - Kaikki's candidate lists both 'crested crane' AND 'giraffe' as senses of the same word (giraffes are noted as not traditionally native to Yorubaland, suggesting an extended/metaphorical use of the crane word). The only real question is the underdot: we have 'àgùnfon', Kaikki has 'àgùnfọn'. Still needs a decision on which spelling to use, just not for the reason first suspected.
definitionCandidateGlosses: a type of long-necked bird with a crest; black crowned crane
giraffe (traditionally, giraffes did not live in the range of Yorubaland)
entries.definitionCurrent: giraffe (traditionally, giraffes did not live in the range of Yorubaland)
